pattern: Increased T2 signal intensity in basal ganglia/thalami in a child

findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is bilaterally symmetric, abnormally increased T2 and FLAIR signal intensity localising to the deep grey matter nuclei, specifically the basal ganglia and thalami. The posterior putamina and ventrolateral thalami are classically and most prominently involved following profound hypoperfusion or anoxia.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include loss of normal grey-white matter differentiation, particularly at the watershed zones, and diffuse cerebral oedema which can cause sulcal effacement. On diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), these affected areas will demonstrate marked restricted diffusion due to acute cytotoxic oedema.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of a focal, unilateral territorial infarction to suggest a large vessel arterial occlusion. Furthermore, there is no intracerebral haemorrhage, midline shift, or primary extra-axial collection to suggest an acute traumatic aetiology.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the striking, symmetrical T2 hyperintensity and restricted diffusion preferentially targeting the highly metabolically active deep grey matter structures in a child.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Hypoxic-ischemic insult (near-drowning episode), given the profoundly symmetric involvement of the basal ganglia and thalami. Deep grey matter is extremely vulnerable to global hypoxia, and severe anoxic events such as near-drowning or cardiac arrest classically manifest with diffuse oedema and this characteristic deep nuclear signal change.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Ischemia, however focal arterial ischaemia typically respects a specific vascular territory rather than presenting with such profound bilateral symmetry. While profound global ischaemia from other causes, such as extreme dehydration or profound hypoperfusion, can produce identical deep grey matter changes, the specific acute anoxic mechanism of near-drowning perfectly accounts for this profound hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Carbon monoxide poisoning, given it also causes symmetric basal ganglia abnormalities, and missing ongoing environmental exposure could put the patient and their family at severe risk. However, carbon monoxide poisoning characteristically produces symmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintensity predominantly isolated to the globus pallidi, whereas a profound global hypoxic insult extensively involves the thalami and posterior putamina as well.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to promptly communicate the extent of the deep grey matter injury and any loss of grey-white matter differentiation to the clinical team, as this indicates a poor neurological prognosis.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to assess the evolution of the cerebral oedema, noting that early swelling may progress to devastating global hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy or eventually chronic encephalomalacia and cortical atrophy.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the brain with diffusion-weighted imaging if the patient was initially assessed only with a non-contrast CT, as this modality is superior here for detecting early cytotoxic oedema and accurately delineating the full extent of the profound hypoxic-ischaemic insult.
management.mriSequences: I would specify diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) and ADC maps, which are highly sensitive for early ischaemic changes and will show bright signal and corresponding low signal respectively, alongside FLAIR and T2-weighted sequences to map the extent of basal ganglia, thalamic, and cortical involvement.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Paediatric Neurology and Intensive Care MDT. My report would clearly document the specific involvement of the thalami and posterior putamina, the degree of cortical watershed restricted diffusion, and the severity of generalised cerebral oedema to guide neuroprotective strategies and prognostic discussions with the family.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is bilaterally symmetric, abnormally increased T2 and FLAIR signal intensity localising to the deep grey matter nuclei, specifically the basal ganglia and thalami. The posterior putamina and ventrolateral thalami are classically and most prominently involved following profound hypoperfusion or anoxia. Supporting features include loss of normal grey-white matter differentiation, particularly at the watershed zones, and diffuse cerebral oedema which can cause sulcal effacement. On diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), these affected areas will demonstrate marked restricted diffusion due to acute cytotoxic oedema. Importantly, there is no evidence of a focal, unilateral territorial infarction to suggest a large vessel arterial occlusion. Furthermore, there is no intracerebral haemorrhage, midline shift, or primary extra-axial collection to suggest an acute traumatic aetiology. The feature that most influences my thinking is the striking, symmetrical T2 hyperintensity and restricted diffusion preferentially targeting the highly metabolically active deep grey matter structures in a child.

The appearances are most in keeping with Hypoxic-ischemic insult (near-drowning episode), given the profoundly symmetric involvement of the basal ganglia and thalami. Deep grey matter is extremely vulnerable to global hypoxia, and severe anoxic events such as near-drowning or cardiac arrest classically manifest with diffuse oedema and this characteristic deep nuclear signal change. The principle differential would be Ischemia, however focal arterial ischaemia typically respects a specific vascular territory rather than presenting with such profound bilateral symmetry. While profound global ischaemia from other causes, such as extreme dehydration or profound hypoperfusion, can produce identical deep grey matter changes, the specific acute anoxic mechanism of near-drowning perfectly accounts for this profound hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Carbon monoxide poisoning, given it also causes symmetric basal ganglia abnormalities, and missing ongoing environmental exposure could put the patient and their family at severe risk. However, carbon monoxide poisoning characteristically produces symmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintensity predominantly isolated to the globus pallidi, whereas a profound global hypoxic insult extensively involves the thalami and posterior putamina as well.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to promptly communicate the extent of the deep grey matter injury and any loss of grey-white matter differentiation to the clinical team, as this indicates a poor neurological prognosis. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to assess the evolution of the cerebral oedema, noting that early swelling may progress to devastating global hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy or eventually chronic encephalomalacia and cortical atrophy. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the brain with diffusion-weighted imaging if the patient was initially assessed only with a non-contrast CT, as this modality is superior here for detecting early cytotoxic oedema and accurately delineating the full extent of the profound hypoxic-ischaemic insult. I would specify diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) and ADC maps, which are highly sensitive for early ischaemic changes and will show bright signal and corresponding low signal respectively, alongside FLAIR and T2-weighted sequences to map the extent of basal ganglia, thalamic, and cortical involvement. This should go to the Paediatric Neurology and Intensive Care MDT. My report would clearly document the specific involvement of the thalami and posterior putamina, the degree of cortical watershed restricted diffusion, and the severity of generalised cerebral oedema to guide neuroprotective strategies and prognostic discussions with the family.
